content.text: This section establishes the framework for developing and maintaining external partnerships that enhance student learning opportunities and prepare students for post-secondary success. Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to creating meaningful pathways that connect students with universities, employers, cultural institutions, and alumni networks, ensuring graduates are well-prepared for their chosen futures. The external partnerships and student pathways program supports the achievement of Schoolwide Learner Outcomes (SLOs), particularly Academic Excellence, Global Competence, Communication, and Innovation, while aligning with CIS Domain F (Community Partnerships) and WASC Category E (School Culture and Support for Student Learning).
The following principles guide all external partnerships and pathway programs:

items: Student-Centered: All partnerships prioritize student benefit, learning outcomes, and future opportunities
Ethical Engagement: Partnerships maintain transparency, avoid conflicts of interest, and uphold school values
Equity and Access: All students, regardless of background or financial circumstances, have access to pathway support
EAL-Inclusive: Programs accommodate students whose first language is not English, providing appropriate support and resources
Data-Informed: Decisions are based on student outcomes, destination tracking, and partnership effectiveness data
Sustainable: Partnerships are built for long-term mutual benefit rather than short-term gains
content.title: 23.1.1 Guiding Principles

content.text: External partnerships encompass:

items: Higher Education Institutions: Indonesian and international universities, polytechnics, and vocational institutions
Industry and Business: Companies offering internships, work experience, and career insights
Professional Organizations: Bodies providing certifications, competitions, and professional development
Cultural Institutions: Museums, galleries, libraries, and cultural centers supporting educational programs
NGOs and Community Organizations: Partners supporting service learning, community engagement, and social impact
Alumni Network: Graduates contributing through mentorship, career guidance, and school support
content.title: 23.1.2 Scope of External Partnerships

content.text: The university counseling program provides comprehensive support for students preparing for tertiary education, ensuring they are equipped to navigate Indonesian and international university admissions processes successfully.

Cross-Reference: Section 12.6 (College Counsellor Role), Job Description 06 (Director of College Counselling), Job Description 15 (College Counsellor) Director of College Counselling (Eduversal Level)

items: Develops network-wide university counseling strategies
Coordinates with College Counsellors across partner schools
Fosters partnerships with universities and admissions organizations
Monitors network-wide university placement outcomes
Organizes cross-school workshops and university fairs
content.title: 23.2.1 Program Structure and Roles

content.text: College Counsellor (School Level)

items: Guides individual students through the university application process
Conducts one-on-one counseling sessions with students and parents
Organizes school-level workshops, university visits, and information sessions
Monitors application progress and outcomes for school students
Coordinates with class teachers on academic preparation and documentation
Maintains relationships with university admissions representatives

content.text: Secondary School Principal

items: Oversees integration of university counseling with academic programs
Supports College Counsellor in resource allocation and scheduling
Reviews university placement outcomes as part of school quality metrics

content.text: The university counseling program follows a structured timeline integrated with the Cambridge Pathway: Grade 7-8 (Lower Secondary) ![][image2] Grade 9 (IGCSE Year 1) ![][image3] Grade 10 (IGCSE Year 2) ![][image4] Grade 11-12 (AS/A Level) ![][image5]
content.title: 23.2.2 Counseling Program Timeline

content.text: Schools provide comprehensive support for major university application systems: UCAS (Universities and Colleges Admissions Service \- UK)

items: Personal statement development with 4,000-character guidance
Reference writing following UCAS conventions
Predicted grade preparation and submission
Interview preparation for Oxbridge and competitive courses
Deadline management (October 15 for Oxbridge/Medicine; January 31 standard)
content.title: 23.2.3 Application Platform Support

content.text: Common Application (USA)

items: Essay writing support for main essay and supplements
Activities list development and description
Counselor recommendation and school profile
Financial aid application guidance (CSS Profile, FAFSA basics)
Early Decision/Early Action strategy

content.text: Indonesian National Systems

items: SNBP (Seleksi Nasional Berdasarkan Prestasi): Academic ranking and portfolio preparation
SNBT (Seleksi Nasional Berdasarkan Tes): Test registration and preparation support
Mandiri (Independent Selection): University-specific application guidance
Raport/transcript preparation according to Indonesian requirements

content.text: Other Systems

items: Direct university applications (Australia, Singapore, Europe)
Portfolio development for art, design, and architecture programs
Audition preparation for performing arts programs

content.text: Given the significant EAL population (40-70%) in Eduversal Partner Schools, the counseling program includes specific provisions:

items: English proficiency test preparation (IELTS, TOEFL, Duolingo) integrated into program
Essay support with awareness of language development stages
University recommendations that consider EAL context and progress
Information about foundation/pathway programs for students requiring additional preparation
Bilingual parent communication (English and Bahasa Indonesia)
Connections with universities offering strong EAL support services
content.title: 23.2.4 EAL Considerations in University Counseling

content.text: Cross-Reference: Section 14.5 (Records Retention Schedule) Required documentation:
content.title: 23.2.5 Counseling Documentation and Records

content.text: Career guidance prepares students for the world of work, whether through direct employment, vocational training, or university-to-career pathways.
Career education is integrated throughout the secondary curriculum: Lower Secondary (Grade 7-8)

items: Career awareness through curriculum connections
Introduction to career sectors and job families
A guest speaker program from various professions
Field trips to workplaces and industry sites
content.title: 23.3.1 Career Education Framework

content.text: Upper Secondary (Grade 9-10)

items: Career exploration aligned with IGCSE subject choices
Aptitude and personality assessments (e.g., Holland Codes, MBTI)
Job shadowing opportunities
Introduction to entrepreneurship

content.text: Advanced Level (Grade 11-12)

items: In-depth career research and planning
Work experience placements
Industry mentorship program
Career fair participation and networking

content.text: Work experience placements provide students with authentic workplace learning opportunities. Program Objectives:

items: Develop employability skills and professional behaviors
Apply academic learning in real-world contexts
Explore career interests and inform future decisions
Build professional networks and references
content.title: 23.3.2 Work Experience Program

content.text: Placement Guidelines: ![][image7] Placement Preparation:

items: Pre-placement workshop on workplace expectations
CV and cover letter development
Interview skills practice
Health and safety briefing
Understanding workplace culture and professional behavior

content.text: Post-Placement Activities:

items: Reflection journal/report
Presentation to peers
Thank you letter to host organization
Evaluation of placement for future students

content.text: Schools develop partnerships with local and national businesses to support career education: Partnership Types: ![][image8] Partnership Development Process:

steps: Identify potential partners aligned with school values and student interests.
Initial approach and relationship building
Partnership proposal with clear benefits and expectations
MOU development (see Section 23.7 for template)
Program implementation and monitoring
Annual review and renewal
content.title: 23.3.3 Industry Partnerships for Career Development

content.text: Schools develop formal partnerships with universities to enhance student opportunities: Partnership Benefits:

items: Guaranteed interview or conditional acceptance programs
Campus visits and taster sessions for students
Professor guest lectures and academic enrichment
Scholarship programs for Eduversal students
Research collaboration opportunities
Recognition of Cambridge qualifications
content.title: 23.4.1 University Partnerships

content.text: Priority Partner Categories: ![][image9] University Visit Program:

items: Minimum 4 university representative visits per year
Annual education fair (hosted or attended)
Campus visit program for Grade 11 students
Virtual university sessions for international institutions

content.text: Partnerships with professional bodies support student development and career preparation: Types of Professional Partnerships: ![][image10] 23.4.3 NGO and Community Partnerships Partnerships with non-governmental and community organizations support service learning and social responsibility: Service Learning Integration:

items: Partnerships aligned with SLO: Ethical Leadership
Student-led community service projects
Integration with Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies (Berkebinekaan Global, Gotong Royong)
Reflection and documentation of learning
content.title: 23.4.2 Professional and Industry Organization Partnerships

content.text: Partnership Guidelines:

items: Organizations must align with school values
Clear safeguarding protocols for student involvement
Supervision requirements for off-campus activities
Insurance coverage for student volunteers
Documentation of hours and activities for student records

content.text: The alumni network is a valuable resource for current students and the school community, providing mentorship, career guidance, and community connection.
Cross-Reference: Teacher Guidebook (Alumni Coordinator Role) Alumni Coordinator Responsibilities:

items: Maintain comprehensive alumni database with current contact information
Plan and execute alumni engagement events
Coordinate mentorship program matching alumni with current students
Facilitate alumni contributions to school programs (guest speaking, work experience)
Manage alumni communications (newsletters, social media, updates)
Track and celebrate alumni achievements
Support fundraising and development initiatives
Coordinate with school administration on alumni involvement
content.title: 23.5.1 Alumni Program Structure

content.text: Alumni Association:

items: Formal structure with elected board
Annual general meeting
Working committees (events, mentorship, fundraising, networking)
Constitution and governance aligned with school policies

content.text: Program Structure:

items: Matching based on career interests, university pathway, or personality
Minimum commitment: 6 months
Communication: Monthly contact (virtual or in-person)
Activities: Career advice, university guidance, skill development
Supervision: Coordinated by College Counsellor and Alumni Coordinator
content.title: 23.5.3 Alumni Mentorship Program

content.text: Mentor Guidelines:

items: Complete mentor orientation and safeguarding briefing
Maintain appropriate professional boundaries
Communicate through school-approved channels
Report any concerns to school coordinator
Provide feedback on mentee progress

content.text: Mentee Guidelines:

items: Prepare for meetings with questions and goals
Respond to communications promptly
Respect mentor's time and professional commitments
Complete program evaluation

content.text: Cross-Reference: Section 14 (Data Protection) Database Contents:

items: Contact information (with consent)
Graduation year and program
University attended and degree
Current profession and employer
Engagement history
Skills and interests for mentorship matching
content.title: 23.5.4 Alumni Data Management

content.text: Privacy and Consent:

items: Alumni consent required for inclusion in database
Option to control visibility of information
Annual data verification and update request
Compliance with data protection requirements

content.text: Cross-Reference: Section 13.4 (Procurement Ethics) Guiding Principles:

items: The school maintains fair and transparent procurement policies
Alumni-owned businesses receive equal opportunity to compete for contracts
Selection based on objective criteria (qualifications, experience, pricing)
No preferential treatment based solely on alumni status
Conflict of interest declaration required when alumni involved in procurement
content.title: 23.5.5 Alumni and Business Relationships

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to ensuring that financial constraints do not prevent students from accessing higher education opportunities.
Types of Scholarships: ![][image12] Counselor Support for Scholarships:

items: Maintain updated database of scholarship opportunities
Notify eligible students of relevant scholarships
Provide application guidance and essay review
Coordinate recommendation letters
Track application outcomes
content.title: 23.6.1 Scholarship Information and Support

content.text: For students applying to international universities, counselors provide guidance on:

items: Understanding total cost of attendance (tuition, living, travel)
Types of financial aid (grants, loans, work-study)
Application processes (FAFSA, CSS Profile, institutional forms)
Negotiating financial aid packages
Comparing offers across universities
Understanding visa implications of financial status
content.title: 23.6.2 Financial Aid Awareness

content.text: Schools may offer internal support programs:

items: Tuition assistance for qualifying families
Emergency education fund for crisis situations
Textbook and materials support
Transportation assistance
Examination fee support (Cambridge, standardized tests)
content.title: 23.6.3 School-Based Financial Support

content.text: Cross-Reference: Section 13.2 (Fee Policy), Section 11.4 (Financial Aid in Admissions)
All formal external partnerships require appropriate approval: 23.7.2 Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) Template Formal partnerships are documented through MOUs containing: Essential MOU Elements:

steps: Parties: Full legal names of school (Yayasan) and partner organization
Purpose: Clear statement of partnership objectives
Scope: Specific activities and programs covered
Duration: Start date, end date, renewal provisions
Responsibilities: Detailed obligations of each party
Resources: Financial contributions, in-kind support, staffing
Intellectual Property: Ownership of materials created
Confidentiality: Data sharing and privacy requirements
Safeguarding: Child protection commitments
Liability: Insurance requirements, indemnification
Termination: Conditions for ending partnership
Dispute Resolution: Process for addressing disagreements
Signatories: Authorized representatives of each party
content.title: 23.7.1 Partnership Approval Process

content.text: Cross-Reference: Section 19, Template T-36 (Partnership MOU Template)
All partnerships are reviewed annually: Review Criteria:

items: Achievement of stated objectives
Student participation and outcomes
Resource utilization and cost-effectiveness
Alignment with school values and strategic priorities
Stakeholder feedback (students, parents, staff)
Safeguarding compliance
Partner satisfaction
content.title: 23.7.3 Partnership Review and Evaluation

content.text: Review Process:

steps: Data collection (participation, outcomes, feedback)
Partner meeting to discuss performance
Written evaluation report
Decision: Continue, modify, or terminate
Documentation and communication of decision

content.text: Schools track graduate destinations to evaluate program effectiveness and inform improvement: Data Points Collected: ![][image14] Collection Timeline:

items: Initial: At graduation (intended destination)
6-month follow-up: Confirmed enrollment/employment
Annual survey: 1, 3, 5 years post-graduation
content.title: 23.8.1 Destination Data Collection

content.text: Analysis Areas:

items: University acceptance rates by institution type
Scholarship success rates
Correlation between academic performance and destinations
Impact of counseling program on outcomes
Comparison across cohorts and schools
content.title: 23.8.2 Destination Data Analysis and Use

content.text: Use of Data:

items: Program evaluation and improvement
Marketing and school profile
Accreditation evidence (CIS/WASC, Cambridge)
Parent and student information
Strategic planning

content.text: Cambridge Learner Destination Tracking: Schools participate in Cambridge's destination tracking to contribute to global data on Cambridge learner outcomes.
Internal Reporting:

items: Annual destination report to Board and Yayasan
Termly updates to SLT on application progress
Data included in school self-study for accreditation
content.title: 23.8.3 Reporting and Communication

content.text: External Communication:

items: Summary statistics in school profile and marketing
Success stories (with alumni consent)
Aggregate data in accreditation submissions

content.text: Privacy Considerations:

items: Individual destinations not published without consent
Aggregate data used for public reporting
Compliance with data protection requirements
University placement data (3-year trend)
Sample counseling records (anonymized)
Partnership MOUs (active)
Career fair programs and evaluations
Work experience placement records
Alumni database statistics
Mentorship program documentation
Student and parent satisfaction surveys
Destination tracking reports
content.title: 23.10.3 Evidence Portfolio Contents

content.text: ## ![][image22] — End of Section 23 — For related information, see:

items: Section 8 (Inclusion and EAL) \- EAL support in university preparation
Section 11 (Admissions) \- Transition support for incoming students
Section 12 (Staff Lifecycle) \- College Counsellor professional development
Section 16 (Community Engagement) \- Parent communication about pathways
Section 19 (Templates) \- Partnership MOU template, counseling record forms
